presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: Fever, cough and difficulty breathing for the past few days
I've been coughing up yellow mucus and feel terrible
Fever and chest pain that's worse when I breathe
I've had chills, a cough, and I can't catch my breath
Feeling very unwell with a productive cough and fever

history_data.hpi: Patient presents with a 4-day history of productive cough with yellow-green sputum, high fever, rigors, and progressive breathlessness. Symptoms began with a mild sore throat and myalgia, then worsened significantly over 48 hours with onset of pleuritic right-sided chest pain — sharp and worse on inspiration and coughing. Fever has persisted despite paracetamol. The patient is finding it increasingly difficult to complete sentences and reports marked fatigue and reduced oral intake.
patient_quotes: I started with what felt like a cold, then it got much worse.
I have a sharp pain on the right side when I breathe in deeply.
I've been bringing up thick yellow-green phlegm.
The fever just won't come down.
I feel absolutely exhausted — I can barely get out of bed.
pmh_pool: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 6 years ago, on metformin
Hypertension — diagnosed 9 years ago, on amlodipine
Former smoker — quit 5 years ago, 15 pack-year history

social_history.smoking: Ex-smoker — quit 5 years ago, 15 pack-year history
social_history.exercise: Normally active — walking daily; now housebound due to illness

negative_history: No TB contacts or TB risk factors
No recent hospitalisation or healthcare exposure
No aspiration risk — no dysphagia, not bed-bound
No immunosuppressive therapy or known immunodeficiency
No leg swelling or calf pain

respiratory: Reduced chest expansion — right lower zone
Dullness to percussion — right lower zone
Bronchial breath sounds — right lower zone
Coarse crackles — right lower and mid zones
Increased vocal resonance and tactile vocal fremitus — right lower zone

investigations.Chest X-Ray: Right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. Cardiac silhouette normal size. Appearances consistent with lobar community-acquired pneumonia. No evidence of cavitation.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 18.4 × 10⁹/L (elevated — neutrophilia), Neutrophils 15.8 × 10⁹/L, Hb 13.2 g/dL, Platelets 322 × 10⁹/L. Marked neutrophilia consistent with bacterial infection.
investigations.CRP: 184 mg/L — significantly elevated, consistent with acute bacterial infection.
investigations.ESR: 76 mm/hr — elevated, consistent with active infection and inflammation.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 132 mmol/L (hyponatraemia — common in pneumonia, especially Legionella), K 3.7 mmol/L, Creatinine 108 µmol/L (mildly elevated — pre-renal dehydration), Urea 9.2 mmol/L (elevated — CURB-65 scoring: Urea >7 mmol/L = 1 point). Rehydration required.
investigations.Blood Glucose: 11.8 mmol/L — elevated, consistent with stress hyperglycaemia in known diabetic. Monitor closely; infection worsens glycaemic control.
investigations.LFTs (Liver Function Tests): ALT 42 IU/L (mildly elevated), ALP 98 IU/L (normal), Bilirubin 18 µmol/L (mildly elevated), Albumin 30 g/L (hypoalbuminaemia — acute phase response). Mild transaminitis — can occur with systemic infection.
investigations.Blood Culture: Two sets sent prior to antibiotics — results pending at 48 hours. Blood cultures positive in approximately 10–15% of CAP cases. Positivity rate higher in severe CAP and bacteraemic pneumococcal disease.
investigations.Sputum Culture: Purulent sputum sent for MC&S prior to antibiotics — results pending. Most common causative organisms in CAP: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Legionella pneumophila.
investigations.ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 116 bpm. No acute ST changes. No right heart strain pattern. Tachycardia consistent with fever and sepsis physiology.

hidden_diagnosis: Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) — Right Lower Lobe, Moderate Severity (CURB-65 Score 2–3)

patient_rules.emotional_state: unwell and fatigued — visibly miserable, slightly anxious about how quickly symptoms have worsened

essential_questions: pleuritic chest pain — location, relationship to breathing
dyspnoea — at rest or exertional, change from baseline
recent hospitalisation or healthcare exposure — aspiration or hospital-acquired risk
travel history — Legionella risk (hotels, air conditioning, spa pools)
vaccination status — pneumococcal and influenza vaccines
essential_examinations: general assessment — unwell, febrile, flushed, rigors
auscultation — bronchial breathing, coarse crackles, reduced air entry
vocal resonance and tactile fremitus — increased over consolidation
mental status — confusion is a severity marker (CURB-65)
critical_actions: recognise community-acquired pneumonia from clinical and radiological features

calculate CURB-65 severity score: Confusion (1), Urea >7 mmol/L (1), Respiratory rate ≥30/min (1), Blood pressure systolic <90 or diastolic ≤60 (1), Age ≥65 (1) — score 0–1: home treatment; 2: consider admission; 3–5: hospital admission, consider HDU
obtain chest X-ray to confirm consolidation and exclude complications
send blood cultures × 2 and sputum culture BEFORE starting antibiotics
start empirical antibiotics within 4 hours of diagnosis — do not delay for culture results
mild-moderate CAP: amoxicillin 500mg–1g oral TDS ± clarithromycin 500mg oral BD for atypical cover
severe CAP: co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS + clarithromycin 500mg IV BD or levofloxacin monotherapy
oxygen therapy: titrate to SpO2 94–98% (or 88–92% if known CO2 retainer)
IV fluid resuscitation if signs of dehydration or sepsis
monitor blood glucose closely in diabetic patients — infection causes hyperglycaemia
reassess at 48–72 hours — if not improving, broaden antibiotics or investigate for complications (empyema, effusion, abscess)
red_flags: CURB-65 score ≥3 — high severity, consider HDU/ICU
Confusion or altered mental status — score 1 on CURB-65, indicates severe disease
Urea >7 mmol/L — indicates severity and renal impairment
Multilobar or bilateral consolidation on CXR
Failure to improve after 48 hours of antibiotics — consider resistant organism, empyema, or alternative diagnosis
Immunocompromised patient — atypical organisms, PCP pneumonia
Age ≥65 with comorbidities — higher mortality risk
acceptable_differentials: Pulmonary embolism with infarction — pleuritic pain and dyspnoea, but no fever or purulent sputum; CXR usually normal
Pleural effusion — dullness on percussion but absent rather than bronchial breath sounds; no consolidation on CXR
COPD exacerbation — wheeze, no consolidation, background COPD history
Lung abscess — cavitating lesion, longer history, foul sputum; seen on CT
Pulmonary tuberculosis — chronic course, night sweats, haemoptysis, upper lobe involvement; no rigors or acute illness
Heart failure — bilateral crackles, orthopnoea, elevated BNP, cardiomegaly on CXR
management_actions: IV access and bloods: FBC, CRP, U&E, LFTs, blood glucose, blood cultures × 2
Chest X-Ray — confirm consolidation and assess severity
CURB-65 severity scoring to guide admission and antibiotic route
Empirical antibiotics: amoxicillin 1g oral TDS + clarithromycin 500mg oral BD (moderate CAP); escalate to IV if severe
IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl if dehydrated or septic (Urea elevated, reduced oral intake)
Blood glucose monitoring — 4-hourly in diabetic patients
Review blood and sputum culture results at 48 hours — narrow antibiotics if organism identified
Reassess clinically at 48–72 hours — if not improving, arrange CT chest to exclude empyema or abscess
Chest X-Ray at 6 weeks post-discharge to confirm resolution — especially in smokers (exclude underlying malignancy)
Pneumococcal and influenza vaccination advice at discharge
critical_history_questions: When did you first feel unwell and how has it changed?
Do you have a fever — have you measured your temperature at home?
Are you coughing up phlegm — what colour is it?
Do you have chest pain — where exactly, and does it change with breathing or coughing?
How breathless are you — at rest or only on exertion?
Did it start like a cold before getting worse?
Has anyone around you been unwell recently?
Have you been in hospital or a healthcare setting recently?
Have you travelled recently — any hotels, spas, or air-conditioned environments?
Do you smoke or have you smoked in the past?
Do you have diabetes, lung disease, or any condition affecting your immune system?
Are you on any steroids or other immunosuppressive medications?
Have you had a pneumonia vaccine or flu vaccine?
Any confusion or difficulty thinking clearly?
